input.prompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the
JSON output.

input.initialPrompt:
I'm starting a new console app on .NET 11 and I need to serialize this record to JSON:

```csharp
public record Person(string name, int age);
```

In the output JSON the property names should be PascalCase (so `Name` and `Age`,
not `name`/`age` or `firstName` style). I want to use whatever the framework
provides out of the box rather than writing my own naming-policy class.

Please show me the full program targeting `net11.0` and run it so I can see the

data.reasoningText:
The user wants a .NET 11 console app that serializes a `Person` record to JSON with PascalCase property names using built-in framework features.

In .NET 11 (which would be .NET 9's successor... let me think about the timeline. .NET 9 shipped Nov 2024, .NET 10 would be Nov 2025, .NET 11 would be Nov 2026. We're in May 2026, so .NET 11 would be in preview.

Actually, let me think about what's available. The user is asking about `net11.0`. Let me check what's actually available. Since we're in May 2026, .NET 11 might be in preview. Let me just try to create the project and see what happens.


System.Text.Json has had several naming policies available over time. CamelCase has been around since .NET Core 3.0, and .NET 8 added snake case and kebab case variants. However, there's no built-in PascalCase policy, so I'll need to create a custom one or find another approach.

The real issue here is that when you define a record with positional parameters like `public record Person(string name, int age)`, the compiler generates properties with the exact same casing as the parameters—so you get lowercase `name` and `age` properties. When serializing this record with System.Text.Json, I need to handle the mismatch between the lowercase property names and whatever casing convention I want in the JSON output.

The user wants PascalCase in the JSON (`Name` and `Age`), so I need to apply a naming policy. I'm trying to recall whether `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` exists—I know .NET 8 added snake_case and kebab-case variants, but I'm not certain about PascalCase being available in earlier versions. Let me just attempt using it and see if it works in the target framework.

My plan is to check what .NET SDKs are available, create a console app for .NET 11, and try using `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` directly. If that doesn't exist, I can fall back to either using `[JsonPropertyName]` attributes or restructuring the record to use PascalCase property names from the start.

data.content: .NET 11 Preview 3 SDK is available. Let me create the project and check if `JsonNamingPolicy.PascalCase` is available out of the box.

arguments.command: mkdir -p /tmp/sv-eb4c2e79b6b441f9bd1e68b7da06e47d/PersonApp && cd /tmp/sv-eb4c2e79b6b441f9bd1e68b7da06e47d/PersonApp && dotnet new console --framework net11.0 --force -o . 2>&1
